macro_segments.title: Summary of Previous Mishna: Liability of Shomrim When Items Break

micro_segments.title: Three positions on shomer liability: Rabbi Meir, Rabbi Yehuda, and Rabbi Elazar
Rabbi Yehuda's middle position: shomer sachar pays, shomer chinam exempt
Rabbi Elazar's challenge: why should shomer chinam be exempt if negligent
Tosafot's question on adam hamazik liability despite shomer status
Ramban's answer: shomer has different legal categorization than adam hamazik

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Yochanan's Takana: Oath as Solution to Shomer Negligence

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yochanan's explanation: oath is a takana to protect workers
Economic rationale: without oath protection, no one would work as shomer
Nature of the oath: I did not break it intentionally, not I was not negligent
Rava's clarification: oath excludes intentional damage, assumes negligence

macro_segments.title: Case Applications: Isi ben Yehuda's Requirement of Witnesses

micro_segments.title: Case of barrel broken on protruding beam in street: Rava requires witnesses
Rav Yosef confirms ruling follows Isi ben Yehuda: no oath if witnesses available
Case of agent buying wine that turned to vinegar: rumor as circumstantial evidence
Rav Yosef confirms: agent must bring proof, cannot rely on oath if evidence available

macro_segments.title: Cases of Half and Full Negligence in Carrying Burdens

micro_segments.title: Agra case: burden too heavy for one person but not requiring two trips, pay half
Dilga case: burden clearly meant for two people done by one, pay full

macro_segments.title: Story of Rabbah bar bar Chana's Workers and the Ethics of Withholding Wages

micro_segments.title: Workers break barrel of wine, employer seizes their cloaks for payment
Rav permits withholding wages: go beyond strict law to help poor workers
Discussion of lifnim mishurat hadin versus din in protecting worker interests

macro_segments.title: New Perek: Hiring Workers (Hassocher et Ha'Poalim) and Working Hours

micro_segments.title: Early and late work: minhag hamedina determines hours unless explicitly stipulated
Tosafot's reading: only binding if stipulated at hiring, not if added after
Feeding workers: minhag hamedina applies to amount and quality of food
Story of Rabbi Yochanan ben Matya: feeding obligation reflects worker dignity
Rabban Shimon ben Gamliel: all terms follow minhag hamedina

macro_segments.title: Working Hours from Sunrise to Nightfall: Reish Lakish's Verse Interpretation

micro_segments.title: Reish Lakish: worker's entry (to work) is his time, exit is employer's time
Practical application and discussion of when worker arrives and leaves
Psalm 104:22-23: animals and humans work during daylight, rest at night
Interpretation: workday runs from sunrise to tzeis, travel times on respective accounts

macro_segments.title: Practical Determination of Work Hours in New Cities and Custom-Based Practice

micro_segments.title: New city with diverse population: follow scriptural parameters or explicit agreement
Rava's drash: Psalm 104 teaches that righteous receive reward in world to come

macro_segments.title: Story of Rabbi Elazar bar Rabbi Shimon and the King's Policeman

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Elazar teaches king's official how to identify thieves by behavior
Official becomes king's policeman seizing Jewish criminals
Rabbi Yehoshua ben Korcha challenges: how can you give Jews to death
Rabbi Elazar responds: I am removing thorns from God's vineyard
Rabbi Yehoshua: that is God's job, not yours to enforce

topical_tags.term: Shomer chinam vs shomer sachar liability
Rabbi Elazar's objection to shomer exemption
Rabbi Yochanan's takana for oath protection
Isi ben Yehuda on witnesses requirement
Rava's application of Isi ben Yehuda
Negligence levels: ones, pshia, gneiva ve'aveida
Reish Lakish's rule on work hours
Sunrise to tzeis as workday span
Psalm 104 on animal and human work
Rabbi Yehoshua ben Korcha's ethical objection
